scenario: Chatwoot work session - audit the Inbox contract before a teardown rework

map_quality.rationale: Hands the agent a usable map: Rails 7 layout with the standard app/ directories, the enterprise/ overlay mechanism, and Inbox located at app/models/inbox.rb:42 as the channel/conversations/contacts hub. The shared concerns and enterprise modules are all named with paths, so the agent can pick an entry point with only small lookups.

specificity.rationale: Paths are concrete throughout — `app/models/inbox.rb:42`, `app/models/concerns/channelable.rb:6`, `reportable.rb:3`, `avatarable.rb:3`, `out_of_offisable.rb:3`, `account_cache_revalidator.rb:1`, `inbox_agent_availability.rb:1`, plus inbox.rb:269-271 for the enterprise stitch-in. No vague references.

justification.rationale: Explains WHY: Inbox is the join between account and channel, channels hang off via Channelable polymorphic `has_one :inbox`, conversations/contacts/messages attach to inbox, and enterprise extends OSS via `prepend_mod_with`/`include_mod_with` rather than forking. Could go deeper on why concerns are layered the way they are.

uncertainty.rationale: No explicit flags about layers it didn't verify — e.g. whether each concern's behavior was actually opened, or how the three enterprise stitch-ins compose. The agent has no signal about which assertions are unconfirmed.

relationship.rationale: Connecting relationships are stated, not just locations: channel `has_one :inbox` polymorphically via `Channelable`, conversations/contacts hang off the inbox, enterprise extension is via `prepend_mod_with`/`include_mod_with`, and three enterprise modules stitched in at `inbox.rb:269-271`. The concerns are listed without per-concern relation prose, which is a minor gap.

steps.step: Map the Inbox contract and what it is built on

map_quality.rationale: Comprehensively maps the contract: every concern with its file:line and what it adds, every association with line numbers (57-76), enterprise overlay, audit hook, and callbacks. The agent has what it needs to preserve the contract with only targeted lookups.

specificity.rationale: Every piece is named with file:line (e.g., `out_of_offisable.rb:3`, `inbox.rb:60`, `enterprise/app/models/enterprise/concerns/inbox.rb:5-10`) rather than vague layer-talk; each association includes line and cascade mode.

justification.rationale: Closing teammate paragraph explains WHY it matters (polymorphic channel is owning, mixed destroy vs destroy_async, enterprise rides the same lifecycle, after_commit fires on destroy). Could go deeper on why concerns vs direct definition, but the why-of-the-contract is there.

uncertainty.rationale: No flags about parts not opened (e.g., didn't confirm `cache_keys` mentioned in the rubric, or whether `InboxAgentAvailability` overrides were fully read). Doesn't say what it didn't verify.

relationship.rationale: Relations are stated explicitly throughout: `belongs_to :channel, polymorphic, dependent: :destroy`, `has_many :contacts, through: :contact_inboxes`, enterprise `has_one :captain_inbox`, audit `associated_with: :account`. The chain from concern → behavior and association → cascade is connected, not just listed.

steps.step: Trace how an inbox and its attached data are torn down

map_quality.rationale: Full chain with file:line from controller trigger → DeleteObjectJob.perform → purge_heavy_associations (inline batches) → destroy! callbacks split into inline vs async → after_destroy/after_commit → enterprise overlay. Agent can follow this path directly.

specificity.rationale: Concrete file:line throughout (`inboxes_controller.rb:78`, `delete_object_job.rb:6/:10/:11`, `inbox.rb:60/:71/:80`, `account_cache_revalidator.rb:5`, enterprise overlay `:10/:14/:4`), each named association tied to its dependent option.

justification.rationale: Explicitly explains WHY purge_heavy_associations runs first ("pre-empts the destroy_async fan-out for the big tables") and why the controller hands off to a job, though the broader 'avoid slow/locking inline destroy' rationale is left implicit.

uncertainty.rationale: Flags WhatsApp teardown as best-effort/swallows errors, but doesn't call out anything it could not fully verify about destroy_async semantics or the enterprise audit-log path.

relationship.rationale: Teardown is written as an explicit connected chain (trigger → job → purge_heavy → destroy! → split callbacks → post-deletion tasks) with each branch labelled inline vs async and each association named by its `dependent:` mode, not a bag of files.

steps.step: Audit every dependent of the Inbox contract

map_quality.rationale: The answer enumerates a broad, grouped dependent set across all required areas — controllers, jobs, concerns, hub models, provider services, lib/integration, and enterprise overlay — with file:line throughout (e.g. `app/jobs/delete_object_job.rb:18`, `lib/chatwoot_hub.rb:69`, `app/services/whatsapp/channel_creation_service.rb:59`). Coverage of the scattered, grep-hostile fan-out is strong enough that the agent can review every area with only targeted lookups.

specificity.rationale: Most entries are concrete with file:line plus a short usage phrase ('teardown heavy-association map', '`Inbox.count` in instance metrics', '`create_inbox`'). A few entries (e.g. several concerns under 'Concerns:' and some enterprise files) are named without a line or a usage phrase, weakening per-item specificity.

justification.rationale: Each group has a clear thematic reason and most entries get a one-phrase 'how it uses the inbox' (caches, teardown, IMAP fetch, create_inbox, instance metrics). Justification is thinner on the bulk-listed records and enterprise files where only the path is given.

uncertainty.rationale: The answer does not flag what a static pass can miss (indirect/polymorphic dependents, enterprise overlay completeness, low-confidence callers). No hedge about coverage limits is offered.

relationship.rationale: Many dependents are connected via the actual relation: `has_one :inbox, dependent: :destroy_async` on channelable, `belongs_to :inbox` on conversation, `EventDataPresenter` → `WebhookListener` chain, `inbox_id` foreign key on message, `cache_keys` concern. However a large block of records and several enterprise/provider files are listed by path without naming the association/concern/path that ties them back to Inbox.

steps.step: Trace how inbox availability and identity are guarded

map_quality.rationale: Maps the guards across assignment (`AutoAssignment::AssignmentJob#perform` :26-27 with `Inbox.find_by(id:)` + `return unless inbox`), IMAP fetch (`should_fetch_emails?` :14-23, `should_fetch_email?` :27-29), and periodic maintenance (stale contact inboxes, stale notifications), plus the round-robin guard. The agent can rework teardown with these guards in hand and only small follow-ups.

specificity.rationale: Concrete file:line throughout — `auto_assignment/assignment_job.rb:26-27`, `fetch_imap_email_inboxes_job.rb:14-23`, `fetch_imap_emails_job.rb:27-29`, `inbox_member.rb:35`, `inbox.rb:80` — with the exact check methods named (`find_by(id:)`, `imap_enabled?`, `reauthorization_required?`, `inbox.present?`).

justification.rationale: Implicitly explains why via the `return unless inbox` after `find_by(id:)` (possibly-deleted inbox) and Redis coalescing/mutex framing, but never spells out the enqueue→run gap explicitly as the reason re-confirmation is needed.

uncertainty.rationale: Does not flag any implicit or missing guard (e.g. jobs that assume the inbox still exists, or unverified `dependent:` semantics). Reads as fully confident across all four areas.

relationship.rationale: Connects guards to the lookup path (`Inbox.find_by(id: inbox_id)` revalidation, `channel.inbox.id` dereference, `Inbox.where(id:)` for orphan computation) and ties round-robin upkeep to `after_destroy :delete_round_robin_agents`. Names the enqueue-side coalescing (Redis in-flight token, EMAIL_MESSAGE_MUTEX) but does not explicitly state the enqueue-then-run gap as the relation that makes re-confirmation necessary.

steps.step: Assess the blast radius of the contract change

map_quality.rationale: Covers the full blast radius across teardown path, jobs, concerns, enterprise overlay, services, and read-only consumers with file:line and a 3-tier risk ranking; cites sense_blast stats (58 direct/120 prod/71 specs) so the agent can scope review.

specificity.rationale: Each at-risk dependent is named with concrete file:line (`delete_object_job.rb:18-40`, `channelable.rb:6`, `contact_inbox.rb:34`, `conversation.rb:66/101`) and a one-phrase reason, not vague groupings.

justification.rationale: Explains WHY each tier is risky: teardown path = data corruption/orphans, medium = race with teardown / bulk delete bypassing callbacks, lower = read-only/counts; the two-way cascade between channelable and inbox is called out as the loop-or-orphan risk.

uncertainty.rationale: Flags the assignment/IMAP race as 'tolerated today' and the enterprise concern as 'easy to miss', but doesn't explicitly call out which dependents were surfaced only structurally vs hand-found, so the agent doesn't know where confirmation matters most.

relationship.rationale: Ties risk to relationship — `dependent: :destroy_async` two-way cascade between channelable and inbox, `inbox_id` NOT NULL FK on conversation, bulk `delete_all` bypassing callbacks, EventDataPresenter→WebhookListener path — rather than just listing files with risk labels.

map_quality.rationale: Comprehensive map with the model + concerns + teardown job to edit, dependents grouped by area (controllers/jobs/services/models), and an extensive specs/fixtures list including the missing teardown test on `spec/models/inbox_spec.rb:13-37`. A teammate could land the change from this alone with only small lookups.

specificity.rationale: Nearly every entry has file:line (`app/models/inbox.rb` associations `:57-76`, callbacks `:80-83`; `app/jobs/delete_object_job.rb:18`; `spec/jobs/auto_assignment/assignment_job_spec.rb:88-89`) and concrete spec/fixture paths (`lib/seeders/inbox_seeder.rb`, `lib/test_data/inbox_creator.rb`).

justification.rationale: Mostly explains why files are in the map ("ADD teardown/no-orphan test — none today", "nil-inbox guard", "if attributes/associations move") tying edits to the teardown change, though some entries are bare paths without a per-file rationale.

uncertainty.rationale: Flags the missing teardown test and qualifies fixtures ("if attributes/associations move"), and the JSON's `must_reverify` lists what needs confirmation, but doesn't explicitly call out enterprise-overlay or association-cleanup semantics as least-certain in the step-7 section itself.

relationship.rationale: Connects edits to routes via association/concern paths (channelable.rb:6 inverse cascade, enterprise concern reopen, heavy-assoc batched purge before destroy!) and ties specs to the path they exercise (assignment_job spec for the nil-inbox guard, delete_object_job spec for heavy-assoc purge). A few spec entries are listed without the specific relation they cover.
